attributes.stem:
A 58-year-old woman presents with a five day history of worsening generalised abdominal pain which has started to radiate down her right leg. She reports bruising appearing on her flank three days after the onset of the abdominal pain (Image 1). She does not have any gastrointestinal or genitourinary symptoms. She has not had any falls or other type of injury. Her history includes atrial fibrillation, diabetes and hypercholesteremia, for which she uses warfarin, atorvastatin and metformin. She was recently treated for a urinary tract infection with trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole.

Her blood pressure is 115/78 mmHg, pulse 90/min and temperature 37.2oC. Her abdomen is soft, apart from some tenderness in the right flank. She keeps her right leg flexed and is unable to straighten it because of the pain. A CT scan is performed (Image 2).
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
Based on the information provided, the lack of a trauma history to the area (e.g. a fall or a tear), the history of anticoagulant use and the CT findings of a retroperitoneal collection overlying the psoas muscle, a spontaneous bleed must be suspected. On the background of recent antibiotic use whilst taking warfarin, a coagulopathy is the likely culprit. Not only can antibiotics remove vitamin K-producing bacteria from the intestine, some antibiotics are cytochrome P450-2C9 inhibitors (the enzyme that breaks down warfarin), prolonging the effect of a warfarin dose. Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole, what was used in this patient, is one of those antibiotics. In an already narrow therapeutic window, small changes to any component of the cascade can result in clinically singificant changes to INR and therefore bleeding. In this patient, her INR was 3.5 (target 2-3 for non-valvular atrial fibrillation).

Given the presence of abdominal pain and muscle spasms in the psoas muscle, along with the appearance of bruising, suggests that there is an abnormal accumulation of blood in the retroperitoneal area, likely a coagulopathic retroperitoneal haematoma due to the factors described above. The diagnosis of retroperitoneal bleed would need to be confirmed through imaging studies such as CT scan or MRI and by ruling out other causes of abdominal pain and bleeding. It is difficult to quanitify the exact location/organ resposible for the bleed without imaging. Usually this is managed conservatively, particularly if there is no blush seen on CT angiogram. Even with a blush, often all that is done is correct the coagulopathy and perform serial haemoglobin measurements.

Acute pancreatitis is unlikely to be the cause of the pain or bleeding in this area. Typically, acute pancreatitis presents with acute severe epigastric pain radiating to the back, often associated with nausea, vomiting and fever. Given the chronicity of this patient's pain and symptoms, this is an unlikely diagnosis. Acute pancreatitis is able to cause an acute retroperitoneal bleed (due to vascular degradation from enzymatic leaks), however it is a relatively rare complication (1-10%).

Other causes for a retroperitoneal collection must be considered. Whilst her abdominal pain could be due to appendicitis and abscess formation, this would not explain the flank bruising. Likewise, the mass could be related to a malignant process, such as a caecal cancer invading posteriorly, but again, this would not explain the bleeding.

A leaking abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) must certainly be ruled out in this patient due to the consequences of missing it. Usually presenting with abdominal pain that radiates to the back, typically in smokers and males >65y/o, a leak may result from a pre-existing AAA on which the vascular wall integrity has been breached. Although a AAA rupture is usually fatal quickly without treatment, a small chronic leak may expand into a large haematoma over a period of days and present as a more acute-on-chronic back, and on occasion abdominal, pain. However, given the patient is a female, under 65 and a non-smoker, this is a less likely diagnosis than a coagulopathic event.
learningPoints: Antibiotic use can affect INR stability in patients taking warfarin
references: Sorensen C. Serious bleeding events due to warfarin and antibiotic co-prescription in a cohort of veterans. The Journal of Emergency Medicine. 2014;47(5):617.
Hosseini M, Hosseinzadeh A, Raufian K, Hedjazi A. Nontraumatic retroperitoneal hematoma after Warfarin Administration. American Journal of Forensic Medicine & Pathology. 2015;36(4):245–8.
Evans RPT, Mourad MM, Pall G, Fisher SG, Bramhall SR. Pancreatitis: Preventing catastrophic haemorrhage. World Journal of Gastroenterology. 2017;23(30):5460.
Rubio M, Rodriguez M, Patnaik S, Wang P. Spontaneous iliopsoas tendon tear. Geriatric Orthopaedic Surgery & Rehabilitation. 2016;7(1):30–2.
Sakalihasan N, Michel J-B, Katsargyris A, Kuivaniemi H, Defraigne J-O, Nchimi A, et al. Abdominal aortic aneurysms. Nature Reviews Disease Primers. 2018;4(1).
Sheikh Y, Hosn S. Chronic contained rupture of abdominal aortic aneurysm. Radiopaediaorg. 2012;
